fields.name: 07. Exploring opinions and building arguments
fields.content-01:
Introduction (5 minutes)

‍

  • Explain the objectives of the activity and its connection to debate skills.
  • Briefly discuss the importance of critical thinking, active listening, and adaptability.

‍

Activity Setup (5 minutes)

‍

  • Arrange the space with markers for opinions (e.g., Strongly Agree to Strongly Disagree).
  • Share the first topic and explain how the movement and discussion will work.

‍

Discussion Round 1 (10 minutes)

‍

  • Present a statement
  • Students move to the part of the room that reflects their opinion.
  • Facilitate a discussion by asking a few students from each position to explain why they chose their stance.
  • Encourage students to move if a peer's argument changes their perspective.

‍

Discussion Round 2 (10 minutes)

‍

  • Introduce a second statement
  • Repeat the process, highlighting shifts in opinion and reasons for those changes.

‍

Discussion Round 3 (10 minutes)

‍

  • Introduce a third statement
  • Repeat the process

‍

Debrief and Reflection (5 minutes)

‍

  • Ask students to share their thoughts on the activity:
    • What did they learn from listening to others?
    • Was it challenging to change their opinion? Why or why not?
  • Highlight the importance of being open-minded and respectful in debates.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • To develop students' skills in expressing and articulating their opinions.
  • To promote active listening and adaptability in responding to others' perspectives.
  • To build students' confidence in defending or modifying their viewpoints.
  • To introduce the foundations of critical thinking and argumentation.
fields.content-03:
Learning Outcomes

‍

By the end of the workshop, students will be able to:

  • Clearly articulate their opinions and reasons for their stance.
  • Actively listen and respond to differing perspectives.
  • Modify their positions based on new information or arguments.
  • Build confidence in expressing themselves in a group setting.
fields.content-04:
Materials and Tools

‍

  • Open space for students to move freely.
  • Signs for "Strongly Agree," "Somewhat Agree," "Neutral," "Somewhat Disagree," and "Strongly Disagree" to mark areas of the room.
  • A list of engaging and thought-provoking topics relevant to students' lives or vocational fields (examples provided at the end of the lesson).
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Observations during discussions: Are students able to articulate their opinions clearly?
  • Participation: Did students actively engage in the movement and discussions?
  • Reflection responses during the debrief.

‍

Tips and Tricks

‍

  • Use statements relevant to students' vocational fields or daily lives to keep them engaged.
  • Keep the atmosphere supportive and emphasize that there are no "right" or "wrong" answers.
  • Ensure all students have a chance to speak, even if it's just one sentence.
  • Be prepared to facilitate discussion by asking follow-up questions or summarizing points if students struggle.
fields.extra-text:
ATTACHMENT: Examples of statements that can be used

‍

  • "Practical skills are more valuable than theoretical knowledge in today’s workforce."
  • "Vocational students should have mandatory internships during their education."
  • "Students should specialize in a career field earlier in their education."
  • "Uniforms should be mandatory in vocational schools."
  • "Students should have more say in designing their school curriculum."
  • "Social media has a more positive than negative impact on society."
  • "Artificial intelligence will take over most human jobs in the next 20 years."
  • “Globalization has a positive/negative impact on local trades.”
